title: Create OME branded excel output
text: Show results Maths Year 7:well maths across class, school sex. wanted can use survey questions demographic columns see relationship two questions.
code: wb = OMESurvey::to_sheet_single_survey_question(data = data,                                                 question_column = 5,                                                 demographic_columns = 2:4,                                                 sheet = 'Table 1') openxlsx::saveWorkbook(wb, file = 'example1.xlsx') wb = OMESurvey::to_sheet_single_survey_question(data = data,                                                 question_column = 5,                                                 demographic_columns = 6:8,                                                 sheet = 'Table 2') openxlsx::saveWorkbook(wb, file = 'example2.xlsx')

title: Create OME branded excel output
text: Choose theme create summary table survey responses theme. (theme must allowable values, .e. agree, disagree, etc)
code: wb = OMESurvey::to_sheet_theme_summary(data = data, theme = 'Theme 1') openxlsx::saveWorkbook(wb, file = 'example3.xlsx')

what: An excel document across all questions
title: Create OME branded excel output
text: can use functions make excel workbook multiple sheets (one question), add hyperlinking table contents sheet (using add_TOC_sheet()).
code: question_columns = 5:15 demographic_columns = 2:4 wb <- openxlsx::createWorkbook() for(i in 1:length(question_columns)){   wb = to_sheet_single_survey_question(data = data,                                        question_column = question_columns[i],                                        demographic_columns = demographic_columns,                                        sheet = paste0('Table ',i),                                        wb = wb   ) }  # Add TOC sheet (with hyperlinks to individual tables) questions = names(data)[question_columns] |>   stringr::str_replace_all('__',': ') |>   stringr::str_replace_all('_',' ') |>   stringr::str_replace_all('XX','?')  wb = add_TOC_sheet(wb, link_text = questions) openxlsx::saveWorkbook(wb, file = 'all_questions_with_TOC.xlsx', overwrite = T)

what: Number of responses across questions in a theme
title: Create survey figures
text: case can use plot_theme() create horizontal bar chart. Depending “Kind” argument can create ggplot, plotly figure formatted data frame.

title: Create survey figures
text: ggplot plotly data.frame
code: OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot') OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'plotly') df = OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'data.frame') df |> DT::datatable(extensions = 'Buttons',                   rownames = F,                   options = list(scrollY = 300,                                  dom = 'Bt',                                  buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel', 'pdf'),                                  paging=FALSE,                                  ordering=F))

title: Create survey figures
text: Note use default survey_values = OMESurvey::survey_values used find expected values survey questions order show answers legend. custom survey values can change survey_values. function uses colours OMESurvey::get_OME_colours(). plots either ggplot plotly object returned, therefore + |> can used update plot (e.g layout/colours, etc). can create plot data frame output. example want swap bar order keep colourscheme.  Note warning given answers questions align survey values. unexpected answers set NA (ggplot).
code: # Use custom survey_values with the order of the values reversed p = OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot',                       survey_values = list(agree = c("Agree a lot", "Agree a little", "Neither",           "Disagree a little", "Disagree a lot") |> rev()))  # Get OME colours colo = get_OME_colours(n = 5, type = 'contrast')  # Swap to the original colour order p + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = colo |> rev(), guide = ggplot2::guide_legend(reverse = TRUE))  #> Scale for fill is already present. #> Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale. data_issue = data data_issue$Theme_1__Question_1[1] = 'Ohh Dear...'  # Use custom survey_values with the order of the values reversed OMESurvey::plot_theme(data_issue, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot') #> Warning in OMESurvey::plot_theme(data_issue, theme = "Theme_1", kind = #> "ggplot"): The expected answers are: "Agree a lot", "Agree a little", #> "Neither", "Disagree a little", "Disagree a lot" which does not include the #> following found in the data: "Ohh Dear..."

what: Number of responses across questions in a theme with a demographic
title: Create survey figures
text: Need add demographic column extract school bar code.
code: school = data$Barcode_ID |> as.character() |>  substr(3, 5) data$school = school school_column = ncol(data)

title: Create survey figures
text: ggplot plotly data.frame
code: OMESurvey::plot_theme_by_demographic(data, theme = 'Theme_1', demographic_column = school_column,  kind = 'ggplot') OMESurvey::plot_theme_by_demographic(data, theme = 'Theme_1', demographic_column = school_column,  kind = 'plotly') # Get school information df = OMESurvey::plot_theme_by_demographic(data, theme = 'Theme_1', demographic_column = school_column,  kind = 'data.frame')  df |> DT::datatable(extensions = 'Buttons',                   rownames = F,                   options = list(scrollY = 300,                                  dom = 'Bt',                                  buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel', 'pdf'),                                  paging=FALSE,                                  ordering=F))

title: Create survey figures
text: can also add OME logo ggplot objects using add_logo(). Note however use gridExtra::grid.arrange() function add logo margin causes object longer ggplot object. Thus, use ‘+’ change plot adding logo.
code: # Add logo. OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot') |> OMESurvey::add_logo() # Add logo, speciify position. OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot') |> OMESurvey::add_logo(position = 'top right') # Add logo, speciify position and sizing arguement. OMESurvey::plot_theme(data, theme = 'Theme_1', kind = 'ggplot') |> OMESurvey::add_logo(position = 'top left',logo_sizing = c(0.3, 0.5))

what: Formatted bar charts from data column
title: Create survey figures
text: can use plot_bar() create bar charts single column within survey data.

title: Create survey figures
text: ggplot plotly data.frame
code: OMESurvey::plot_bar(data, column = 5, kind = 'ggplot') #> Warning: The `size` argument of `element_line()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the OMESurvey package. #>   Please report the issue to the authors. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was #> generated. OMESurvey::plot_bar(data, column = 5, kind = 'plotly') df = OMESurvey::plot_bar(data, column = 5, kind = 'data.frame') df |> DT::datatable(extensions = 'Buttons',                   rownames = F,                   options = list(scrollY = 300,                                  dom = 'Bt',                                  buttons = c('copy', 'csv', 'excel', 'pdf'),                                  paging=FALSE,                                  ordering=F))

title: Create survey figures
text: Note can include NA bar charts using passing arguments table() via .... Moreover, ggplot outputs can include numbers plot y setting showValue = TRUE.
code: OMESurvey::plot_bar(data, column = 5, kind = 'ggplot', showValue = TRUE, useNA = 'always') #> Scale for y is already present. #> Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale.

title: dave_plotting_functions
text: summary_plot_stacked_bar() summarises several categorical questions one horizontal stacked bar chart. variables representing questions must share response scale. three attitude questions use Likert scale.  couple things note : vector colours colo simply taken one questions. (something fancier, hard.) need select() variables use data frame pass plotting function. function treats every variable dataframe receives question plotted, hence need select() first. , course, use base R equivalent: survey_data[,c("q_satisfaction", "q_belonging", "q_support")] |> summary_plot_stacked_bar(...). questions can also ordered according response pattern using order_values option. example, can order proportion respondents answering "Agree" "Strongly agree". example also show extract details (question labels well colour palette) validation_df rather hard-coding .
code: attitude_labels <- c(   q_satisfaction = "I am satisfied with the programme",   q_belonging = "I feel that I belong on the programme",   q_support = "I know where to get support if I need it" )  survey_data |>   dplyr::select(q_satisfaction, q_belonging, q_support) |>   summary_plot_stacked_bar(     colo = validation_df |> filter(variable=="q_satisfaction") |> pull(colo) |> unlist(),     labels_vec = attitude_labels,     count_style = "both",     titleText = "Attitudes to the programme"   ) attitude_vars <- c("q_satisfaction", "q_belonging", "q_support")  # find labels that match vars attitude_labels <- validation_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable %in% attitude_vars) |>   dplyr::arrange(match(variable, attitude_vars)) |>   dplyr::pull(item_statement) |>   stats::setNames(attitude_vars)  # find colour palette attitude_colo <- validation_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable == attitude_vars[1]) |>   dplyr::pull(colo) |>   unlist()  # plot survey_data |>   dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(attitude_vars)) |>   summary_plot_stacked_bar(     colo = attitude_colo,     labels_vec = attitude_labels,     count_style = "both",     order_values = c("Agree", "Strongly agree"),     titleText = "Attitudes ordered by positive responses"   )

title: dave_plotting_functions
text: three helpfulness questions use response scale, denominator. Financial-support helpfulness relevant respondents used financial support, academic-support helpfulness relevant respondents used academic support, welcome-week helpfulness asked everyone. Just plotting variables bit misleading respect conditions/routing.  Simply removing missing values na.rm=TRUE option gives nicer-looking plot misses point somewhat: missing / non-allowed values routing, several .  plot improvement, properly capture 20 substantive responses, 5 missing/invalid responses, 55 non-routed non-responses. summary_plot_stacked_bar() function supports “extended” data format deal , plotted variable name ending _value companion variables ending _include, _plot: *_value: response value plot; *_include: whether respondent included question’s denominator; *_plot: whether response valid substantive value show. can construct _value, _include, _plot variables like, ’s function make_extended_summary_plot_data() automatically. Provide data, validation information variables want work gives dataframe ready passing summary_plot_stacked_bar(). ’s can set helpfulness questions.  Note especially label “(20/25)” financial support question, capturing 20 substantive responses (5 missing/invalid/non-allowed responses) amongst 25 respondents routed question. true academic support question. welcome week support question, asked everyone, still full number respondents indicated, reflecting asked everyone thus condition data dictionary.
code: survey_data |>   janitor::tabyl(q_finance_support_helpful, q_finance_support_used) |>   janitor::adorn_title() |>   kable_narrow() helpful_vars <- c(   "q_finance_support_helpful",   "q_academic_support_helpful",   "q_welcome_support_helpful" )  # Get labels and colours from validation_df helpful_labels <-   validation_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable %in% helpful_vars) |>   dplyr::arrange(match(variable, helpful_vars)) |>   dplyr::pull(item_statement) |>   stats::setNames(helpful_vars)  helpful_colo <-   validation_df |>   dplyr::filter(variable == helpful_vars[1]) |>   dplyr::pull(colo) |>   (\(x) x[[1]])()  # plot survey_data |>   dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(helpful_vars)) |>   summary_plot_stacked_bar(     labels_vec = helpful_labels,     colo = helpful_colo,     count_style = "both",     titleText = "Helpfulness of support",   ) survey_data |>   dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(helpful_vars)) |>   summary_plot_stacked_bar(     labels_vec = helpful_labels,     colo = helpful_colo,     na.rm = TRUE,     count_style = "both",     titleText = "Helpfulness of support"   ) # set up variables to work with helpful_vars <- c(   "q_finance_support_helpful",   "q_academic_support_helpful",   "q_welcome_support_helpful" )  # build the extended data for plotting helpful_ext <- make_extended_summary_plot_data(   data = survey_data,   validation_df = validation_df,   vars = helpful_vars )   #plot helpful_ext |>   summary_plot_stacked_bar(     dat_format = "extended",     labels_vec = helpful_labels,     colo = helpful_colo,     na.rm = TRUE,     count_style = "both",     titleText = "Helpfulness of support"   ) knitr::knit_exit()

title: Boxplot (horizontal), in an OME style. — OME_boxplot_
text: data data frame. value_var Numeric variable plot. group_var Optional grouping variable (factor). show_counts Logical; whether display (valid/total) counts right-hand axis. Defaults TRUE. na.rm Logical; whether remove/ignore missing values. na.rm = TRUE, count labels show number plotted (.e. non-missing) observations; na.rm = FALSE, labels show non-missing total number observations. valueLabText Optional title value variable axis. NULL (default) title removed; "" name value_var used. groupLabText Optional title group variable axis. NULL (default) title removed; "" name group_var used. omitGroupLabels Optional logical controlling whether omit group labels. Helpful group_var=NULL. Default FALSE. titleText Optional plot title. colour Boxplot outline colour (valid R colour). Defaults get_OME_colours. separate_at Optional integer specifying draw horizontal separation line groups defined group_var. positive n separation line first n categories negative n separation line n-th last category. Default NULL 0 omits line. group_label_width Optional integer. Width (characters) used wrapping group labels stringr::str_wrap(). Default NULL, wrap. group_labels Optional named character vector providing alternative labels grouping variable. form c(level1 = "Label 1", level2 = "Label 2"). Default NULL, uses factor's existing levels. ... Additional arguments passed OME_boxplot() OME_boxplot_(). used calling OME_boxplot_() directly.

text: dat data frame. response_var Bare column name giving response categories (used fill). group_var Optional bare column name defining separate bars. facet_var Optional bare column name used faceting. variables factors (characters may work guaranteed). percCut Numeric scalar (0-100). Cutoff percentages shown bar segments. Default 5; use number >100 suppress percentages. colo Optional character vector fill colours (one per response level). NULL (default), colours taken OMESurvey::get_OME_colours(type = "distinct"). na.rm = FALSE, grey colour prepended "Missing" level. na.rm Logical. TRUE, remove NA responses; FALSE (default) convert "Missing" treat additional response level. NA_label Character, default "Missing". Label use NA responses. show_counts Logical; whether display (n) counts bar. Defaults TRUE. Needs care used faceting. count_style Character string controlling counts displayed show_counts = TRUE. Options : "non-missing": show number non-missing responses "total": show total number responses "": show "(non-missing/total)" Defaults "non-missing" na.rm = TRUE, otherwise "". horiz Logical (default FALSE). TRUE, flip coordinates bars horizontal place legend plot. text_scale Positive number (default 1) scaling size percentage labels. fillLabText Optional legend title fill variable. NULL (default) title removed; "" name response_var used. groupLabText Optional label bar/group axis. NULL label removed; "" name group_var used. propLabText Label proportion axis. Default "Proportion responses". titleText Optional plot title. Default NULL removes title. facet_labels Optional named character vector used labeller facets (names original facet values, values labels display). Default NULL uses raw facet values. facet_layout Optional character. "1row" "1col" facets arranged single row column. Otherwise (NULL) default facet layout used. title: Plot stacked Likert-style survey questions (ROME style) — plot_ROME_survey
text: data data frame selected column survey question values categorical responses (character factor). NAs treated non-responses excluded per-question N. columns Character integer vector selecting question columns; order matters (bars/facets appear top→bottom order). Default NA uses columns data. ordering Optional character vector giving desired response order -positive -negative. Controls bar stacking legend order (legend display reversed first level appears left bar). percentage_cutoff Numeric [0, 100]; -bar percentage labels smaller suppressed (0-dp, % sign). label_size Numeric text size (ggplot2 size units) -bar percentage labels right-edge N labels. n_label_pad_text Numeric passed hjust N labels; negative values place label slightly outside 100\ values pull inside bar. per_question_in, base_height_in, legend_allowance_in, width_in Sizing controls (inches) used compute recommended figure dimensions stored returned object (see “Value”). y_label_wrap_width Integer; approximate wrap width (characters) question (demographic) labels flipping. legend_wrap_chars Rough character budget per legend row; used choose guide_legend(nrow = ...) long legends wrap cleanly. right_margin_min_pt Minimum right plot margin (points) reserve space right-edge N labels. demographic Optional character scalar naming demographic column data (e.g., "Gender"). supplied, plot facets question (top→bottom order columns) draws one stacked bar per demographic level within question. NULL NA, function falls back plot_ROME_survey_questions().

title: Plot stacked Likert-style survey questions (ROME style) — plot_ROME_survey
text: ggplot object. addition usual plot methods, object carries attributes saving consistent size: attr(p, "width_in") — recommended width inches; attr(p, "height_in") — recommended height inches; attr(p, "rome_dims") — list width, height, units = "".

title: Plot stacked Likert-style survey questions (ROME style) — plot_ROME_survey
text: percent axis spans 0–100 major gridlines every 5\ 0/25/50/75/100. Axis ticks axis lines suppressed. legend placed bottom, left-aligned, can wrap multiple rows according legend_wrap_chars. Per-question N (excluding NA) printed right edge bar. Colours drawn get_OME_colours() number observed response levels. Note: function uses theme settings legend.location = "plot" (honored ggplot2 >= 3.5.0); earlier versions setting ignored, plot still render.

title: Plot stacked Likert-style survey questions (ROME style) — plot_ROME_survey
text: top--bottom order questions follows order columns pass . Internally function fixes factor levels (coord_flip()) visual order matches supplied columns.

title: Plot stacked Likert-style survey questions (ROME style) — plot_ROME_survey
code: # Minimal toy example (creates a small survey-like data frame) if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   lvls <- c("Agree a lot","Agree a little","Neither",             "Disagree a little","Disagree a lot")   make_col <- function(n = 400) {     sample(c(lvls, NA), n, replace = TRUE,            prob = c(0.34, 0.29, 0.20, 0.12, 0.05, 0.00))   }   df <- data.frame(     `Is Jake lovely?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake wonderful?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake just the best?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake the person who most inspires you?` = make_col()   )    # The order of 'columns' controls the top->bottom order in the chart:   cols <- names(df)    p <- plot_ROME_survey_questions(     df,     columns = cols,     ordering = lvls,     percentage_cutoff = 10,     y_label_wrap_width = 40   )   print(p)    # Save with the recommended size:   ggplot2::ggsave(     "rome_demo.png", p,     width  = attr(p, "width_in"),     height = attr(p, "height_in"),     units  = "in", dpi = 300   ) }   if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1)   lvls <- c("Agree a lot","Agree a little","Neither",             "Disagree a little","Disagree a lot")   make_col <- function(n = 400) {     sample(c(lvls, NA), n, replace = TRUE,            prob = c(0.34, 0.29, 0.20, 0.12, 0.05, 0.00))   }    df <- data.frame(     `Is Jake lovely?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake wonderful?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake just the best?`  = make_col(),     `Is Jake the person who most inspires you?` = make_col(),     Gender = sample(c("Male","Female"), 400, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.47, 0.53))   )    cols <- names(df)[names(df) != "Gender"]    p_dem <- plot_ROME_survey_by_demographic(     data = df,     columns = cols,     demographic = "Gender",     ordering = lvls,     percentage_cutoff = 10,     y_label_wrap_width = 30   )   print(p_dem)    ggplot2::ggsave(     "rome_questions_by_gender.png", p_dem,     width  = attr(p_dem, "width_in"),     height = attr(p_dem, "height_in"),     units  = "in", dpi = 300   ) }

code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  # Examples here use scale_colour_OME(); scale_fill_OME() is exactly analogous.  # Prepare example dataset with labelled factors mtcars_small <- mtcars |>   mutate(     am = factor(am, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Auto", "Man")),     cyl = factor(cyl, levels = c(4, 6, 8))   )  # Scatterplot using OME palette & theme for colour aesthetic # (Not OME-like data and illustration would be better if the factor had more than two levels, #  but hopefully illustrates use of scale_colour_OME() when colouring by a factor) mtcars_small |>   ggplot(aes(x = wt, y = mpg, colour = am)) +   geom_point() +   scale_colour_OME() +   labs(     x = "Weight (/1,000 lb)",     y = "Miles per gallon",     colour = "Transmission",     title = "Fuel efficiency vs weight,\ncoloured by transmission type"   )   # Use a sequential palette for an ordered factor ("divergent" is available too) mtcars_small |>   ggplot(aes(x = wt, y = mpg, colour = cyl)) +   geom_point() +   scale_colour_OME(type = "sequential") +   labs(     x = "Weight (/1,000 lb)",     y = "Miles per gallon",     colour = "Cylinders",     title = "Fuel efficiency vs weight,\ncoloured by cylinders"   )

title: Section-level summary plot for numeric questions — summary_plot_boxplot
text: dat tibble/data.frame containing survey responses. function supports two input formats: Simple format: one numeric column per question. questions assumed included plotted. Extended format: columns named using pattern question_value, optional question_include question_plot columns. *_value columns contain numeric responses. *_include (logical) indicates whether response included analysis . *_plot (logical) indicates whether response shown plot (values set FALSE treated missing). missing *_plot *_include columns assumed TRUE. cases, *_value variables numeric. variables used plot included. .e. calls may need form data |> dplyr::select(<variables needed plotting>) |> summary_plot_boxplot(). dat_format One "auto", "simple", "extended". Defaults "auto", detects extended format *_value columns present, otherwise assumes simple format. labels_vec Optional named character vector labels use questions plot. Names correspond variable names (without _value appended extended format), values labels display axis. na.rm Logical. Controls missing values handled plot. Missing values (including created via *_plot = FALSE) : removed TRUE retained (therefore reflected displayed count) FALSE (default). order_fun Function used order questions plot, NULL. function accept arguments (x, na.rm = TRUE) return single numeric value (e.g. median, mean). NULL ordering variables data. Defaults median. titleText Optional text use plot title. group_label_width Optional integer. Width (characters) used wrapping question labels axis. Passed OME_boxplot_(). Default 30. ... Additional arguments passed OME_boxplot_().

title: Section-level summary plot for numeric questions — summary_plot_boxplot
text: ggplot object, horizontal boxplot summarising survey questions.

title: Section-level summary plot for numeric questions — summary_plot_boxplot
text: variables dat pivoted long format, ordered according value returned order_funapplied question's observed responses (withna.rm = TRUE`). original column order dat preserved stable tie-breaker multiple questions identical ordering statistics. Missing-value handling ordering plotting intentionally separated: missing responses ignored ordering purposes, treatment plot controlled na.rm. dat supplied simple format, internally converted extended format *_plot *_include values set TRUE.

title: Section-level summary plot for numeric questions — summary_plot_boxplot
text: Dave Sirl

title: Section-level summary plot for numeric questions — summary_plot_boxplot
code: # Minimal example with three numeric questions dat <- tibble::tibble(   Q1 = c(1, 2, 3, NA),   Q2 = c(5, 4, 3, 1),   Q3 = c(2, 2, 2, 3) )  # Simplest use dat |> summary_plot_boxplot()    # Add question labels labels <- c(   Q1 = "High-valued question",   Q2 = "Low-valued question",   Q3 = "Mid-valued question" ) dat |> summary_plot_boxplot(labels_vec = labels)   # With longer labels labels_long <- c(   Q1 = "Question where responses tend to be at the higher end of the scale",   Q2 = "Question where responses tend to be at the lower end of the scale",   Q3 = "Question where responses tend to be somewhere in the middle" ) dat |> summary_plot_boxplot(labels_vec = labels_long)   # Control label wrapping summary_plot_boxplot(   dat,   labels_vec = labels_long,   group_label_width = 20 )   # Remove missing values for plotting dat |> summary_plot_boxplot(na.rm = TRUE)   # Extended format example dat_ext <- tibble::tibble(   Q1_value = dat$Q1,   Q1_plot = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),   Q2_value = dat$Q2,   Q2_include = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) ) summary_plot_boxplot(dat_ext)

text: dat tibble/data.frame containing survey responses. function supports two input formats: Simple format: one factor column per question. questions assumed included plotted. Extended format: columns named using pattern question_value, optional question_include question_plot columns. *_value columns contain factor responses. *_include (logical) indicates whether response included analysis . *_plot (logical) indicates whether response shown plot (values set FALSE treated missing). missing *_plot *_include columns assumed TRUE. cases: response variables factors identical levels. variables used plot included. .e. calls may need form data |> dplyr::select(<variables needed plotting>) |> summary_plot_stacked_bar(). dat_format One "auto", "simple", "extended". Defaults "auto", detects extended format *_value columns present, otherwise assumes simple format. labels_vec named vector labels use questions plot. Names variable names (without _value appended) values corresponding labels. na.rm Logical. controls whether missing values (including created via *_plot = FALSE) included plot separate category. TRUE, remove NA responses; FALSE (default), convert NA_label treat additional response level. NA_label Character, default "Missing". Label use NA responses. percCut numeric scalar (0-100). Cutoff percentages shown bar segments. Default 5. colo (optional recommended) vector colours use fill scale (character vector colours, length number levels factor first variable dat.) NULL (default) pallete OMESurvey::get_OME_colours(type='distinct') used. na.rm = FALSE grey colour prepended "response" level. Note percentage labels white, pallete needs work . (... function/package needs upgrading!) order_values Optional. Controls questions ordered plot. May NULL, character vector response levels, special value "mean(.numeric())". See Details. titleText Optional text use plot title. fill_label_width Optional integer. Width (characters) used wrapping fill labels stringr::str_wrap(). Passed OME_stacked_bar(). Default 20. group_label_width Optional integer. Width (characters) used wrapping vertical (group/question) axis labels stringr::str_wrap(). Default 30. show_counts Logical; whether display (n) type counts bar. Defaults TRUE. Needs care used faceting. count_style Character string controlling counts displayed show_counts = TRUE. Options : "non-missing": show number non-missing responses "total": show total number responses "": show "(non-missing/total)" Defaults "".

code: # Minimal example with three questions and three response levels dat <- tibble::tibble(   Q1 = factor(c("Yes", "No", "Yes", NA), levels=c("No", "Maybe", "Yes")),   Q2 = factor(c("No", "No", "Yes", "Maybe"), levels=c("No", "Maybe", "Yes")),   Q3 = factor(c("Maybe", "Yes", "Maybe", "Yes"), levels=c("No", "Maybe", "Yes")) )  # Simplest use summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat)   # Add question labels labels <- c(Q1 = "Question 1", Q2 = "Question 2", Q3 = "Question 3") summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat, labels_vec = labels)   # With custom wrapping if they are long labels_long <- c(   Q1 = "Question 1",   Q2 = "Question 2",   Q3 = "The third question asked as part of this series of three questions" ) summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat, labels_vec = labels_long)  summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat, labels_vec = labels_long, group_label_width = 20)   # Remove missing values summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat, na.rm=TRUE)   # Extended format example dat_ext <- tibble::tibble(   Q1_value = dat$Q1,   Q1_plot = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),   Q2_value = dat$Q2,   Q2_include = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) ) summary_plot_stacked_bar(dat_ext)
